title: Why Fatigue Lowers Your Trigger Threshold
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: Fatigue from complex trauma isn't just tiredness; it's a physiological state that lowers your trigger threshold. Learn why rest is essential for healing.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The scene evokes a sense of gentle, deliberate healing and the beauty of a nervous system learning to mend itself. The light is warm and the atmosphere is one of profound peace.
htmlContent:
The low, persistent hum of the refrigerator feels unusually loud tonight. Your keys, dropped on the counter, clatter with the force of a cymbal crash. It’s not that the world has gotten louder... it’s that the insulation inside you, the soft buffer between your nervous system and the raw data of the world, has worn dangerously thin.

This is the territory of a nervous system running on empty, a state of significant fatigue that does more than just make us tired. It fundamentally lowers the threshold for what we perceive as a threat, turning everyday moments into a minefield of potential triggers. This isn’t a failure of will or a lack of resilience; it is the predictable, physiological consequence of a system that has been overdrawn for far too long, a system that has forgotten the deep rhythms of rest and repair. The exhaustion is not merely in the muscles but in the very wiring that interprets the world, a deep, cellular weariness that changes the math of our own survival responses.

The Energetic Economics of Hypervigilance

Imagine a country perpetually at war, its entire economy reoriented to defense. Factories produce tanks instead of tractors, resources are diverted from schools and hospitals to the front lines, and the population lives in a state of constant, low-grade alert. What emerges is the internal economy of a nervous system shaped by complex trauma. the ceaseless, subconscious scanning of the environment for threats... is an incredibly expensive metabolic process. It’s a full-time job that the body was never meant to perform for decades on end, and it carries an invisible but substantial energetic cost that quietly bankrupts our internal resources over time.

When our reserves are this low, the system defaults to its most primitive and efficient survival strategy: to perceive threat and react. It doesn’t have the luxury of complexity or the energetic capacity for a measured response. It simply acts to neutralize the perceived danger, which is why a seemingly small event can trigger a cascade of reactions... a racing heart, a flood of cortisol, a desperate urge to flee or fight. We are not overreacting; we are reacting from a place of deep energetic poverty, where every stimulus is potentially the one that will push us over the edge. Understanding this is not an excuse, but an explanation - a compassionate and accurate reframing of a phenomenon that is too often misunderstood as personal failure. We can't simply will ourselves to have more energy; the work is not in forcing but in slowly, deliberately, beginning to change the underlying economic conditions of our own internal world. This begins with recognizing the cost of the war and daring to question if it's a war we still need to be fighting.

Neuroception: The Body’s Silent Sentry

Long before the conscious mind has time to form a story about what is happening, the nervous system has already made its own assessment. This subconscious process of threat detection, a concept brilliantly articulated by researcher Stephen Porges, is called neuroception. It is the reason why a certain tone of voice can make our stomach clench, or why a crowded room can feel suffocating, even when we consciously believe we are safe. Neuroception is not concerned with what is logical; it is concerned with what feels familiar to past experiences of harm.

That becomes a significantly disorienting experience, one that can lead to a deep sense of alienation from our own reactions. We feel ourselves pulling away from connection, lashing out in ways that create more isolation, all while a part of us watches, bewildered. A client once described this to me as feeling like a passenger in a car being driven by a terrified child. It involves practices that help restore the energetic resources the nervous system needs to upgrade its detection software, moving from a pixelated, black-and-white view of the world to one that can perceive color, depth, and complexity. It is about providing the system with new data, new experiences of safety and connection, so that its neuroceptive process can become more accurate and less biased toward a past that is no longer happening. We might find some useful perspectives on this in articles exploring how the body keeps an internal record of our experiences.

For those dealing with fatigue from chronic stress, consider Thorne Vitamin B Complex (paid link).

The Collapse of the Window of Tolerance

The concept of the "window of tolerance" describes the optimal zone of arousal in which we can function most effectively. Inside this window, we are able to respond to the demands of life with a sense of groundedness and flexibility. We can think clearly, manage our emotions, and connect with others. For those with a history of complex trauma, this window is often quite narrow to begin with. Chronic fatigue, however, causes this window to shrink even further, sometimes to the width of a razor’s edge.

Wild, right?

The Feedback Loop of Fatigue and Fear

Fatigue and fear exist in a self-perpetuating cycle. When we are exhausted, our perception of threat is heightened, which in turn activates the sympathetic nervous system’s fight-or-flight response. We become trapped in a feedback loop where the symptom (fatigue) exacerbates the cause (a dysregulated nervous system), and the cause reinforces the symptom. Breaking this cycle requires interrupting it at one of its key points - not by trying to eliminate fear, but by addressing the physiological state that amplifies it.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

Interrupting this cycle is not about positive thinking or forcing ourselves to calm down. It is about introducing moments of genuine physiological rest and safety. This could be as simple as placing a hand on the heart and feeling the warmth, spending five minutes focusing on the sensation of the breath, or looking at a calming image. These small, seemingly insignificant actions are potent signals to the nervous system. Over time, these moments accumulate, creating a buffer that allows the prefrontal cortex to stay online more of the time. It allows us to meet a stressful moment with a degree of choice, a sliver of space between stimulus and response. It turns out to be a central theme in many healing modalities, including those discussed in articles on parts work and internal family systems. The goal is not to silence the fear, but to cultivate a state of being that is spacious enough to hold it without being consumed by it.

The Architecture of Deep Rest

In a culture that glorifies productivity and pathologizes rest, the act of cultivating deep, restorative quietude is a radical one. We have been taught to equate rest with inactivity, with laziness, with a failure to be productive. But true rest is not simply the absence of activity; it is a potent and dynamic physiological state, a vital nutrient for a depleted nervous system. For those of us recovering from complex trauma, learning the architecture of deep rest is not an indulgence, but a non-negotiable aspect of our healing. It is the slow, patient work of rebuilding our internal home on a foundation of safety and energetic sustainability.

Cultivating this state involves a portfolio of practices, not a single magic bullet. It might involve restorative yoga, where the body is fully supported by props, allowing the muscles to release their chronic holding patterns. It could be non-sleep deep rest (NSDR) protocols, which guide the mind into a state of real relaxation. It might be as simple as spending time in nature, allowing the fractal patterns of leaves and the gentle sounds of birdsong to soothe the nervous system. As explored in some psychological publications, the benefits of mindfulness are well-documented for stress reduction. For further reading, you can find many articles on sites like Psychology Today that explore into these topics. The key is consistency and a willingness to experiment, to find the unique combination of practices that signal safety to your specific system. It is about treating rest not as a reward for hard work, but as the very foundation from which all meaningful work and life can emerge. It is about understanding that our capacity to engage with the world is directly proportional to our capacity for stillness. That becomes a core principle in understanding how to manage emotional flashbacks and other trauma symptoms.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does fatigue make me so irritable?

Irritability is a classic sign of a nervous system in a state of sympathetic activation, or fight-or-flight. When striking fatigue depletes your energetic resources, your system has a much lower capacity to manage sensory input and emotional stress. This results in a shortened fuse, where even minor annoyances can feel like significant threats, triggering a disproportionate, defensive response because the body simply lacks the energy for a more measured reaction.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

Bear with me on this one.

Can I just sleep more to fix this?

While adequate sleep is crucial, it often isn't enough to resolve the deep, cellular exhaustion associated with complex trauma. The fatigue is not just from a lack of sleep but from a state of chronic internal hypervigilance that runs 24/7, even during sleep. True recovery requires cultivating deep rest and safety, which involves practices that actively downshift the nervous system into a parasympathetic state, allowing the body to finally stop scanning for threats and begin the work of repair.

The path out of this significant weariness is not a straight line, nor is it a battle to be won through force of will. It is a slow, patient turning toward the body’s own intelligence, a relearning of its native language of sensation and rhythm. It requires us to question the cultural narratives that equate our worth with our productivity and to instead honor the significant wisdom in our need for stillness. The fatigue is a messenger, and it is asking a very simple, very direct question. What would happen if you finally stopped trying to outrun your own need for rest?

A simple grounding stone that fits in your pocket is Worry Stone - Amethyst (paid link).

That is your work now. Not understanding. Doing.
